feedback.text: I strongly support the planned Digital Fairness Act, as it is a long overdue step towards greater fairness in the digital space. Digital products, whether software, music, films or video games, are now a key part of many people’s lives, yet consumers’ rights are often weaker than for physical purchases. Anyone who buys a digital product should be allowed to use it on a permanent basis. Unfortunately, it is happening that content is subsequently rendered unusable, for example because online servers are switched off or DRM systems are deactivated. From the point of view of many consumers, this is unfair, as they have legally purchased the product and suddenly no longer have access to it. The Digital Fairness Act should close this gap and ensure that digital purchases remain usable in the long term, even if the provider stops its service. In this context, the Stop Killing Games initiative has also raised an important issue. When people buy digital games or software, they should not simply be deprived of their use. I believe that the Digital Fairness Act should reflect this principle not as a special rule only for games, but as a general rule for all digital products. This is not about punishing companies, but about trust and responsibility: Anyone who sells a product should ensure that the buyer can continue to use it in the future. This creates long-term customer satisfaction and strengthens the Digital Single Market. It is equally important to me that the Digital Fairness Act promotes honest and transparent communication without unnecessarily hampering creative advertising. On the subject of influencer marketing, I believe that influencers should not be regulated separately or harder than other forms of advertising. Most influencers are not large advertising companies, but individuals who share content in an authentic way. What matters is therefore whether advertising is transparent, clearly labelled and not misleading, and not who publishes it. Digitalisation has brought huge opportunities, but it needs clear and fair rules. The Digital Fairness Act can ensure that European consumers really get what they have paid for in the future without complicated usage conditions, hidden restrictions or loss of purchased content. If Europe takes digital fairness seriously, it can become an international model with a market that strengthens innovation, consumer protection and trust alike.
